nodes.title: Prepare settle functions in outer scope
nodes.why: These references are filled inside the Promise constructor, then exposed to callers.
nodes.alternative: Do not expose settle functions at all.
nodes.tradeoff: External control is great for tests, but it can be misused in production flows.

nodes.title: Create the Promise once
nodes.why: The deferred object should manage one promise instance with one lifecycle.
nodes.alternative: Create a new Promise every time resolve/reject is called.
nodes.tradeoff: Single promise keeps behavior predictable, but each deferred is one-shot.

nodes.title: Capture `resolve` from executor
nodes.why: This lets tests decide exactly when the promise should fulfill.
nodes.tradeoff: More control for tests, but timing now depends on outside calls.

nodes.title: Capture `reject` from executor
nodes.why: Tests can force failure paths without waiting for real network or timers.
nodes.alternative: Throw errors in helper code instead of rejecting.
nodes.tradeoff: Explicit rejection is realistic for async APIs, but callers must handle rejections.

nodes.title: Return the deferred contract
nodes.why: Keeping everything in one object makes test setup and orchestration straightforward.
nodes.alternative: Return an array tuple like [promise, resolve, reject].
nodes.tradeoff: Object return is self-describing, with slightly more property access overhead.
